basic_info.name: Sunscreen UV Filter Compatibility
basic_info.name_ja: 日焼け止めUVフィルター適合性
basic_info.description: HSP distance between organic UV filters (chemical sunscreen actives) and the cosmetic vehicle (emollients, oils, emulsion phases) determines the solubility limit of the UV filter in the formulation, directly affecting achievable SPF, photostability, and product aesthetics. Poor HSP matching leads to UV filter crystallization, reduced SPF, whitening, and instability. Rational HSP-based selection of emollients and co-solvents maximizes UV filter loading and uniform distribution in the film on skin.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Organic UV filters must be dissolved in the cosmetic vehicle at concentrations high enough to achieve the target SPF. The solubility of a UV filter in an emollient or oil phase is governed by the HSP distance between them: a smaller Ra (or RED < 1) indicates the UV filter is within the solubility sphere of the vehicle, predicting good dissolution. When the RED exceeds 1, the UV filter approaches or exceeds its solubility limit, risking crystallization during storage or upon application to skin as volatile components evaporate. For combination sunscreens using multiple UV filters, each filter must be individually soluble in the vehicle. Furthermore, the HSP of the dried film on skin (after volatile components evaporate) determines long-term photostability, as UV filter migration and phase separation in the film reduce effective UV absorption. HSP matching also predicts interactions between UV filters themselves, which can affect photostability through energy transfer mechanisms.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_filter-dD_vehicle)^2 + (dP_filter-dP_vehicle)^2 + (dH_filter-dH_vehicle)^2); RED = Ra/R0_filter; Solubility limit correlates with RED: higher loading possible at lower RED
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for adequate solubility; RED < 0.7 for high UV filter loading and long-term stability

required_inputs: UV filter HSP (dD, dP, dH) for each active ingredient
data_sources: HSPiP database for common solvents and some UV filters
Published UV filter HSP from cosmetic science literature
Group contribution methods for UV filter HSP estimation
Emollient supplier technical literature (Evonik, BASF, Ashland)
Experimental solubility screening in emollient series
complementary_parameters: UV filter molar absorptivity and absorption spectrum
Melting point of UV filter (crystallization tendency)
Water resistance requirements (for W/O or water-resistant formulations)

limitations: HSP predicts thermodynamic solubility but not crystallization kinetics, which depend on nucleation and supersaturation conditions
Skin interaction (sebum, sweat, temperature) alters the effective vehicle HSP after application
Volatile silicones and solvents evaporate after application, changing the film HSP and potentially causing UV filter precipitation
Photodegradation products of UV filters have different HSP, potentially causing phase separation during UV exposure
Emulsion systems add complexity: UV filter partitioning between oil and water phases is not fully captured by simple HSP matching
Polymeric film formers used in water-resistant sunscreens modify the effective HSP environment unpredictably
known_failure_modes: Avobenzone (butyl methoxydibenzoylmethane) crystallization in low-polarity emollients despite apparently adequate RED values, due to strong self-association
UV filter precipitation upon volatile silicone evaporation in spray sunscreens, where the residual film HSP shifts dramatically
Incompatibility between certain UV filter combinations (e.g., avobenzone + octinoxate) driven by photochemical rather than HSP interactions
High-SPF formulations where UV filter concentrations exceed thermodynamic solubility limits even at optimal HSP matching
validation_method: Accelerated stability testing at elevated temperature (40-45C) and freeze-thaw cycling
Polarized light microscopy for UV filter crystal detection
In vitro SPF measurement (ISO 24443, Diffey-Robillard method)
UV filter dissolution rate and concentration monitoring by HPLC

key_references.title: Photostabilization of butyl methoxydibenzoylmethane (Avobenzone) and ethylhexyl methoxycinnamate by bis-ethylhexyloxyphenol methoxyphenyl triazine (Tinosorb S)

literature.regulatory_relevance: UV filter solubility in the vehicle directly impacts regulatory compliance: FDA OTC Monograph (21 CFR 352) and EU Cosmetics Regulation (EC 1223/2009) specify maximum allowed concentrations for each UV filter. HSP-based formulation ensures filters remain dissolved at permitted levels. Stability requirements (ICH-like accelerated stability for cosmetics) demand that UV filters stay in solution throughout product shelf life. COLIPA/COSMETICS EUROPE in vitro SPF methods require uniform UV filter distribution.

commercial_tools: DSM Personal Care UV filter formulation tools
